danger_signals.visual_form: boxer animation shift to aggressive posture
boxer character animation becomes slower, slouched
danger_signals.meaning: player boxer vulnerable to knockouts, reduced defensive capability

danger_signals.visual_form: boxer character knocked to canvas, must recover within round timer
danger_signals.meaning: imminent knockout threat if count reaches completion

danger_signals.visual_form: opponent spinning animation or stance windup (visual tells for special moves)
danger_signals.meaning: incoming high-damage attack that can be countered or avoided

safety_signals.visual_form: transition screen between rounds showing boxer at rest
safety_signals.meaning: brief respite period; player can regenerate stamina via button mashing

safety_signals.visual_form: boxer holding defensive posture without damage taken
player boxer positioned at optimal range for punch execution
safety_signals.meaning: favorable positioning for attack or counter-punch

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: player boxer customizable via stats (punch, stamina, speed); opponent boxers feature distinct visual designs and quirky character names
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: ring floor in arcade features publisher logo (Namcot in JP version, Data East in US version)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: eight unique opponent boxers with distinct character designs: Violence Jo, Brown Pants, White Wolf, Bomba Vern, Beat Brown, Blue Warker, Green Hante, Onetta Yank; each has unique appearance and fighting style
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: opponent identity communicated through character sprite and arcade-style character portraits

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: facing lower-tier opponents (Violence Jo listed as entry-level champion in NES version)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: opponents utilize basic attacks, less aggressive AI patterns

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: progression through ranked opponent roster; UI stats display (MATCHES, WINS, K.O.'S, LOSSES)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: opponent difficulty escalates; higher-ranked boxers employ special attacks more frequently

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: arcade version cycles through final three opponents (Blue Warker, Green Hante, Onetta Yank) indefinitely
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: sustained high-difficulty combat state; endless championship defense loop

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: UI displays three stat categories (punch, stamina, speed) that increase via power points earned post-match
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player boxer attacks and movement become visually faster/more powerful; stamina management becomes less punishing

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: character animation slows noticeably; boxer posture becomes defensive/slouched
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: punch effectiveness reduces; stamina regeneration required via button input between rounds

environmental_storytelling.example: Japanese arcade/NES version features eagle with 'Namcot' logo on ring floor; US NES version replaces with Data East logo—communicates publisher identity and regional ownership through diegetic environment

quirky character monikers (Violence Jo, Brown Pants, White Wolf, Bomba Vern) create distinct personality/identity for each boxer without extensive dialogue; names suggest fighting styles or appearance traits
match statistics display (MATCHES, WINS, K.O.'S, LOSSES) reinforces career progression narrative and player achievement; password save system enables persistent career identity
training gym/training session interface differentiates between versions (NES vs VS. System); visual layout and UI elements communicate player agency in boxer development

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: distinctive animation windup (spinning for hook, stance shift for straight punch, loading pose for uppercut); telegraphs attack type to allow counter-play
reduced animation speed, defensive posture; less aggressive attack frequency
rapid punch combinations; frequent special attack attempts at optimal distance
visibly slower movement; reduced punch speed and range; increased vulnerability to knockouts
character knocked to canvas; must complete recovery animation before returning to neutral stance; failure results in technical knockout
unique animation sequences (spinning hook, rope collision, aerial launch); instant knockout or massive damage on clean hit

notes: Ring King (1987 NES) communicates world-state primarily through character animation, UI statistics display, and opponent roster progression. The game lacks explicit faction/political systems—instead emphasizing individual boxer identity and career advancement. Danger is communicated through animation tells (opponent stance changes, stamina depletion visuals, knockdown states) rather than environmental hazards. Regional differences (JP vs US branding on ring floor) are cosmetic publisher markers rather than diegetic world-state changes. The game's 'comical' tone (per sources) suggests exaggerated animation and character design as primary manifestation tools. Tier T3 data: empirical observation requires actual gameplay footage to verify exact visual manifestation techniques; sources document gameplay mechanics and character roster but lack detailed visual design documentation.
